[[RunningHead]]

Psyche and Society: Explorations in Psychoanalytic Sociology

By Robert Endleman. New York: Columbia University Press, 1981, 448 pp., $30.00.

Ethel Person M.D.

Ethel Person

Ethel Spector Person, M.D.
New York City

Ethel Person  

I had looked forward to reading this book because its title suggested a subject of some interest to me—the problem of how individuals internalize aspects of their culture. Psychoanalytic theory has developed in directions that permit psychoanalytic explorations of that question without resorting to an overly simplistic cultural perspective, in

257

which the assumption is made that the psyche simply mirrors the external world. However, when I turned to the book, my anticipation quickly changed to disappointment and, finally, to disbelief.

Granting that sociobiology has been justly attacked as guilty of reducing social phenomena to biological ones, Endleman suggests that psychoanalysis can bridge the two. Thus, his focus is not, as the title misleadingly suggests, the study of psyche and society; it is an attempt to outline the evolutionary biological underpinnings of psyche and to return to an appreciation of man as an animal. He wishes to offer a "reconstruction not only of the anatomical and physiological evolution of the species, but also of the behavioral and the psychological." This is all well and good, but it should be noted that Endleman is a professor of sociology and a practicing analyst and, therefore, not manifestly qualified to give an authoritative account of human evolution.

In his assumed role as evolutionist, Endleman throws aside methodological constraints implicit in both his parent disciplines. His arguments are presented in well-known ambiguous phrases—could have, must have, may have—and he engages in some truly amazing legerdemain. For example, he cites Goodall's report of a female chimp who, having finished her estrus, became jealous of a new female chimp who was getting too much attention, and began to show the pink swelling of estrus all over again. He interprets this as the female chimpanzee faking estrus receptivity. He goes on to propose the possibility that the smarter hominid female ancestors began extending estrus periods by several days and consequently these smarter, sexier females attracted more male attention and produced more and smarter offspring, with the eventual evolution of nonperiodic continuous sexual receptivity.

Theodore Cohen

Theodore Cohen  

Myron Hofer utilizes sociobiology, ethology, and recent advances in neurosciences to gain insight into human behavior. Although never openly hostile to psychoanalysis, he basically ignores its contributions. When he mentions Sigmund Freud, it is to point out how wrong he was about the psychic determinants of oedipal repression. Hofer prefers Piaget's sensorimotor explanations of cognition. He omits work of Anna Freud, Margaret Mahler, Robert Emde, Eleanor Galenson, etc., although child psychoanalysis and infant psychiatry have much to offer to a dialogue.

[[RunningHead]]

The Annual of Psychoanalysis, Vol. 8, 1980

Edited by The Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis. New York: International Universities Press, 1981, xiii + 359 pp., $25.00.

Eugene Halpert M.D.

Eugene Halpert

Eugene Halpert, M.D.
Great Neck, New York

Eugene Halpert  

It is a difficult task to review an annual, even one with a tradition of high standards and excellence such as The Annual of Psychoanalysis. The great number of authors, the diversity of the content of the various papers (there are eighteen papers grouped under six different headings), as well as the diversity of the audiences or purposes to which the various papers are addressed make general comments and statements inadequate. The only adequate generalization might be that the editorial excellence and high standards of the previous editions have been maintained and that the diversity of papers may offer something to suit a diversity of readers. However, as in any such collection of papers, the quality and value of the contribution and the degree of controversy it may arouse varies.

The very first paper by George Pollock, "Freud as Scientist and Psychoanalysis as Science," was originally presented at the First International Symposium of the Unconscious, held at Tbilisi, Georgia, U.S.S.R., in October 1979. For that audience and purpose it is a clear, well-spoken exposition of analysis as a science that all analysts would probably find familiar and agree with. In contrast, there are five papers which deal primarily with Kohut's theories and their application which would be more controversial. These papers range from one by Roy Schafer, which offers a friendly critique of Kohut and discusses the relation between Schafer's action language and Kohut's psychology of the self, to Ernest S. Wolf's and James Wilson's study of "The 'Monday Crust' in the Disorders of the Self." The readers' response to these five papers would vary according to their views of Kohut's theories.

The section of the Annual on psychoanalytic education contains two particularly worthwhile and stimulating papers. The first is a hitherto unpublished paper of Otto Fenichel's entitled "Theoretical Implications of the Didactic Analysis." Fenichel's paper, which was originally delivered to a group of training analysts at Topeka in 1938, touches on most if not all the major issues and problems of training analysis that continue to confront us to this day and does so in Fenichel's characteristic lucid and concise manner. In the other paper dealing with psychoanalytic education, Stanley S. Weiss and Jean Fleming offer a comprehensive review of the topic of teaching and learning of termination and make suggestions as to how best to teach candidates about termination. Among their suggestions is that every candidate be required to write a brief summary of a terminated case.

Development and Pathology of the Self. The Pchiatric Clinics of North America, 4(3)

Edited by Kenneth Robson. Philadelphia: Saunders, 1981, 147 pp., $42.00 per volume (3 issues per year).

Alan Eisnitz M.D.

Alan Eisnitz

Alan J. Eisnitz, M.D.
New York, New York

Alan Eisnitz  

In his foreword, Robson, as editor, indicates the purpose of the volume—the integration of childhood data relating to self development. He also suggests an emphasis on the theories of Heinz Kohut. Most, but not all the authors follow this emphasis.

Marjorie McDonald, in a brief review of the psychoanalytic concept of the self and the self-representation before Kohut, notes "it is not enough merely to treat the structural conflicts that underlie the neurotic symptoms" (p. 142). Implying thereby a dichotomy between narcissistic and other forms of conflict, including the structural, she belies this view in a second article, on the psychoanalytic treatment of a young boy with a "traumatic psychosis." In this sensitive and most interesting account, she demonstrates the intertwining of the boy's object-related, instinctually derived conflicts with the structural regression connected with his shattered self.

Anna Ornstein gives a concise and extremely lucid summary of Kohut's views on development, and then applies these to childhood disorders. She believes most childhood psychopathology represents disorders of the self, the result of the parents' inability to offer themselves as adequate "selfobjects.

669

[[RunningHead]]

Personality Disorders: Diagnosis and Management (2nd Ed.)

Edited by John R. Lion. Baltimore: William & Wilkins, 1981, 606 pp., $36.00.

Alex Kaplan M.D.

Alex Kaplan

Alex H. Kaplan, M.D.
St. Louis, Missouri

Alex Kaplan  

The problem of psychoanalytic nosology has confounded all of us in our clinical since the discoveries of Freud. Inconsistencies in

674

our nosological framework have been increasingly noted and inaccuracies have been present so frequently that they have become the rule. Even today our unpreciseness in diagnosis continues. More than that, some analysts believe an initial diagnostic study contaminates the analytic process and others feel the diagnosis is immaterial to the understanding of the patient or the proscribed treatment. On the other hand, there has been an increasing number of analysts who feel that to better understand the specific psychopathology, our psychoanalytic theories and the psychoanalytic treatment process itself, issues of diagnosis cannot be ignored as if there has always been mutual understanding and agreement in all these areas. The pressure for more confirmable diagnoses continues not only in the field of psychoanalysis, but in psychiatry as well, which is now using a neo-Kraepelinian basis for the psychiatric disorders, the 1980 Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM III).

Since the publication of the DSM III, the third diagnostic manual since 1952, considerable efforts have already gone into the planning for the next manual, DSM IV, testifying to the similar confusion in psychiatric nosology. Because of this confusion, psychiatrists point to the need to improve on our ability to categorize psychiatric disorders in order to be able to develop more significant research to tease out etiological factors. But many of the diagnoses in DSM III are couched in statistical and behavioral terminology that gives little understanding of the intrapsychic conflicts, the particular defenses utilized, the influence of the past, and the effect of reality on specific psychopathology. Such knowledge would increase our awareness of the psychodynamic factors involved and lead to a more effective diagnosis, a more accurate evaluation of the degree of ego impairment, and the specific type of treatment indicated.

[[RunningHead]]

The Developmental Approach to Childhood Psychopathology

By Humberto Nagera. New York: Aronson, 1981, 507 pp., $30.00.

Gerald Olch M.D.

Gerald Olch

Gerald Olch, M.D.
Seattle, Washington

Gerald Olch  

The conception of the "developmental approach" of the book's title is used in several ways. One use is in making connections between symptomatic behavior and the successive developmental phases, as in the chapter on sleep disturbances and the chapter on children's reaction to hospitalization. A second use is in the study of a particular phase of development, such as the chapter on the female adolescent and the one on autoerotic actions and ego development. Another use, in comparison of children and adults, appears the simplest, but is sometimes the source of challenging questions, as in the chapter on insight and the chapter on reactions to the death of important objects. Here, use of the developmental approach yields two questions: "At what developmental phase is insight possible?" and "At what developmental phase is mourning possible?"

Nagera sometimes goes outside psychoanalysis to support his conceptions of development. He gives special attention to brain maturation not being achieved until age two to support his belief that day-care arrangements are potentially harmful to infants. There is a valuable twelve-page section on "Brain Development" in the chapter "Day Care Centers," where he reviews the psychological implications of neurological development. The same point is made more briefly in Chapter 2, "The Normal Child," and Chapter 4, "Vulnerability and Stimulation in Early Life." Then, in Chapter 5, "Self and Object," he turns to Piaget's sensorimotor stages of development to support the conception that "genuine" object relations do not develop until the permanent object (in Piaget's sense) is achieved.

The "childhood psychopathology" part of the book's title applies only in the most general sense. Seven of the chapters are on clinical topics: "Sleep Disturbances," "The Imaginary Companion," "Children's Reactions to the Death of Important Objects," Children's Reactions to Hospitalization and Illness," "Day Care Centers" and "The Primary Caretaker Septum: A Method of Running Inpatient Units for Children.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud in Germany. Revolution and Reaction in Science, 1893-1907. Psychological Issues, Monogr. 41

By Hannah S. Decker. New York: International Universities Press, 1977, xi + 364 pp., $28.50 (soft cover, $22.50).

Ernest Kafka M.D.

Ernest Kafka

Ernest Kafka, M.D.
New York, New York

Ernest Kafka  

Decker's book has two purposes. One is historical, the second, critical. As to the historical aspect, Decker describes Freud's early ideas, the situation of the German culture and society, and the psychological, psychiatric, and politico-social thought of the period 1893-1907 in order to examine the German response to early psychoanalysis. The second purpose is to show that Freud's often stated opinion that he was ignored, unappreciated, or reviled at that time was historically inaccurate and that in fact, his writings were read, seriously discussed, and influential. The historical information she gives has a value for those interested in the evolution of psychoanalytic ideas. The political, critical use to which Decker puts the information she presents, to fault Freud, to reveal him as a neurotic, will have a value to those who, while they may or may not be interested in the development of psychoanalysis, have some desire to diminish Freud.

Freud is generally regarded as one of the towering discoverers, revolutionaries, and creators of history. He associated himself with heroic peers: biblical figures such as Joseph and Moses, and historic ones such as Plato and Leonardo. He wrote of them as a colleague would, as forebears, rivals, and human beings resembling himself. He speculated about their personal psychologies as he investigated his own. Freud had Promethean ambitions.

702

[[RunningHead]]

Early Development And Education Of The Child

Charles Mangham M.D.

Charles Mangham

Charles A. Mangham, M.D.
Seattle, Washington

Charles Mangham  

By Willi Hoffer, ed. Marjorie Brierley. New York: Aronson, 1981, xvii + 220 pp., $20.00.

Marjorie Brierley, in collecting and editing these papers, has created

231

a mélange of significant scientific contributions and historical and biographical memoirs from the post-World-War I period. The purpose of this anthology is stated succinctly in Anna Freud's foreword. The collection and publication of an author's writings "more than any other acknowledgment … serve[s] to highlight the significance of a man's work for the discipline to which he has devoted his efforts."

The great division in Hoffer's life and writings occurred at the time of World War II and the exodus from Austria. He had the opportunity to observe and record with movies the infants and children of Miss Freud's war nurseries. His most creative work came from these observational studies of the borderland between biology and psychology and the psychoanalytic conclusions he was able to draw from them. Although the data from these studies are observational, one should keep in mind in reading this book that Hoffer firmly believed that "psychoanalysis is a representational science." These papers reveal his ability to synthesize psychoanalysis and infant observation.

Part I begins with "Mouth, Hand and Ego Integration" (1949) and includes papers and lectures developing the themes established in his first and principal paper on infant observations. Perhaps the single most creative working idea he developed from his observations was that of dual or multiple stimulations, which are self-generated when the infant touches his hand to his mouth. This concept enabled Hoffer to develop his ideas regarding differentiation of self from object and the development of the concept of the primordial ego as being a body ego. In the later papers he touches on the pain barrier and infantile narcissism that protect the infant from self-destructiveness. He presages Sandler's concept of background of safety when he speaks of the self as a "constant" in all interchange with the environment. "When it [the self] loses this constancy, when it disintegrates, the perception of and the relationship with the environment disintegrates and psychosislike states result." He suggests that self-inlicted aggression is a failure in the "double touch system." There is a failure to establish a sense of self, resulting from environmental failure; comforting somehow is not possible by the use of the thumb. The self and the not-self become confused and the baby attacks indiscriminately.

The Annual Of Psychoanalysis, Vol. X.

Warren Goodman M.D.

Warren Goodman

Warren H. Goodman, M.D.
Great Neck, New York

Warren Goodman  

Edited by The Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis. New York: Int. Univ. Press, 1982, xii + 388 pp., $30.00.

This volume is a compendium of some very interesting and provocative papers. It was dedicated to the late Dr. Joan Fleming of the

247

Chicago Institute. Befitting the dedication, a significant portion of the contents of the volume is devoted to psychoanalytic education. There are four major groupings of contributions. The first section is on psychoanalytic education, followed by sections on theoretical studies, psychoanalytic history and applied psychoanalysis. There are 21 individual papers by 24 contributors contained within 388 pages. The volume contains a helpful index and the tables of contents of Volumes I-X of this valuable Annual. Among the papers on psychoanalytic education is one by Jacob Arlow on a psychoanalytic perspective of psychoanalytic education and another by Homer Curtis exploring the educational prerequisites for analytic training. Additional papers on psychoanalytic education are offered by Leo Rangell, Helen Beiser, Victor Calef and a paper on "The Problem of the Probem Candidate," by Stanley Weiss.

Theoretical studies include a paper by Harold Blum on the "Transference in Psychoanalysis and in Psychotherapy" and a "Revision of the Psychoanalytic Theory of Affect" by Pinchas Noy. There are additional theoretical contributions by Samir Stephanos, Robert Stolorow, George Atwood and Robert Holt, as well as a paper by Edwardo Val on "Self-Esteem Regulation and Narcissism."

The section on psychoanalytic history contains a compelling review by Anna Freud of "The Past Revisited" and an interesting perspective by George H. Pollock on "Psychoanalysis in Russia and U.S.S.R., 1908-1979." There is a translation of reminiscences of Sigmund Freud by Bruno Goetz and another translation of a delicious fairy tale written by Bertha Pappenheim called "The Hazel Nut Torte" revealing another facet of the complicated and interesting personality that was Anna O.

Psychoanalysis and Psychiatry: Models for Potential Future Relations

Melvin Sabshin M.D.

Melvin Sabshin

Melvin Sabshin, M.D.
American Psychiatric Association
1400 K Street, N.W.
Washington, D.C. 20005

Melvin Sabshin  

TOWARD THE END OF HIS DISCUSSION OF lay analysis in 1927, Jones stated, "Both the internal and external bonds between psycho-analysis and clinical medicine … are fundamental in character, and they can be ignored only at considerable cost to psycho-analysis" (p. 196; italics added). In my opinion, those words continue to be pertinent today; I would add that the fundamental bonds between psychiatry and psychoanalysis can be ignored only at considerable risk for psychiatry.

Despite the changes in theory, practice, socioeconomic context, and new research during the 56 years since Jones' statement, and despite the tendency to drift apart, especially during the recent political vicissitudes and economic storms, I believe that both psychiatry and psychoanalysis will benefit more from renewal than from discontinuity. Perhaps it is time to negotiate a new contract based on present realities and future potentialities; indeed, many of us believe this is a time for a new beginning rather than a trial separation.

Medical Director American Psychiatric Association.

Presented at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 16, 1983. Accepted for publication November 2, 1984.

Psychoanalysis, Philosophy, and the Public

Theodore Shapiro M.D.

Theodore Shapiro

Theodore Shapiro, M.D.
One East 57th Street
New York, New York 10021

Theodore Shapiro  

During the early weeks of January a series of articles directly affecting the public image of psychoanalysis appeared in the intellectual and popular press—The New York Times(Goleman, 1985), The New Republic(Crews, 1985), New York Review of Books(Lieberson, 1985). All reported on Grünbaum's (1984), The Foundations of Psychoanalysis: A Philosphical Critique. These articles are accorded prominent space even though a philosophical treatise, especially one couched in technical, abstruse language, is not usually of arresting interest to either the editors or the readers of these popular publications. We would suggest that the reason the book has aroused interest is that the editors and readers presume it is a book about psychoanalysis, not a philosophical tract. Moreover, Frederick Crews, the author of the New Republic article, has used the book to suggest that Grünbaum has all but buried psychoanalysis as a viable scientific enterprise. That might be news if it were true!

The book, in fact, is not about the death of our science; rather, it and the former writings by Grünbaum, which have appeared in many reputable psychoanalytic and philosophical journals, are an attempt to look at the central tenets of psychoanalysis and assess their scientific status, given current philosophical definitions (see Laor, 1985). He asks what sort of science is psychoanalysis if a science at all?

[[RunningHead]]

Preface: On Books

Theodore Shapiro M.D.

Theodore Shapiro

Theodore Shapiro, M.D.
Payne Whitney Hospital Cornell University Medical Center
525 East 68th Street
New York, New York 10021

Theodore Shapiro  

THERE IS NO INTELLECTUAL ENTERPRISE in which the gathering of new knowledge and information is important where the reading of books is not a central activity of its practitioners and students. This holds true for psychoanalysis as it does for the humanities, the laboratory sciences, and all the learned professions.

This supplement is a first—an entire issue of the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association devoted to books—yet it is but an extension of a practice already prevalent in the intellectual world, as evidenced by time-honored book review sections of newspapers and literary supplements. Although the Journal has always published book reviews and book essays, there has been an increasing time lag between the publication of a book and its review in the Journal. This supplement is an attempt to catch up and to provide our readers with the latest information so that we can hereafter offer a more timely guide for selecting and reading books pertinent for psychoanalysis.

In our modern world even reading book reviews can become a problem, although the intention of reviewing is to make the task of combing the literature easier. Like the compulsive, we become ever more removed from the primary drive and intention and focus on a displaced behavior. On the other hand, reviewers try to make our reading choices more pointed insofar as they tell other professionals and scholars what there is to be read, and they tell their tales from a vantage point that is personal and creative. A book review offers color and opinion and judgment in addition to a recapitulation of the text in summary form. We can admit to being readers of reviews as well as readers of books, without shame.

A Secret Symmetry. Sabina Spielrein Between Jung And Freud

Martin Silverman M.D.

Martin Silverman

Martin A. Silverman, M.D.
Maplewood, New Jersey

Martin Silverman  

By Aldo Carotenuto. New York: Pantheon, 1982, xix + 250 pp., $16.95.

We stand upon the shoulders of those who have preceded us. Psychoanalysts owe a special debt to the early pioneers who braved the swirling waters and shifting quicksands of the human psyche to map out islands of safety for us to employ in our present-day investigations. The turn-of-the-century picture that emerges from this book is of courageous (and at times foolhardy) men and women whose emotional strength and moral fiber were severely tested by what they unleashed as they plumbed their own and their patients' psychic depths.

The early analysts had only the flickering torches of Freud's initial clinical observations and theoretical constructions to light their way as they conducted their enthusiastic explorations. They were protected from the storms their researches activated within them by self-analyses that were all too brief and much too superficial to provide them with more than an illusion of control over their own human conflicts. As in other fields in which investigators handle dangerous substances, some fell ill themselves and some perished.

[[RunningHead]]

Prisoners of Childhood

By Alice Miller. New York: Basic Books, 1981, 118 pp., $11.95.

Sydney Pulver M.D.

Sydney Pulver

Sydney Pulver, M.D.
Philadelphia, Pennsylvania

Sydney Pulver  

This book was very well received in the popular press. Unfortunately, some of the reasons for this popular reception make it less useful than it might be to the professional reader. The book's simplicity, for example, is both an asset and a liability. I admire the author's clarity, which she achieves mainly by avoiding analytic jargon. In so doing, however, she oversimplifies the real complexity of the psyche, and does this to a degree which often is exceedingly misleading. Since this is damning in a book addressed to clinicians, let me elaborate. The author's theme is a simple one, and owes much to Winnicott and Kohut. (She also gives credit to Mahler, but I find her to be quite distant from what I would consider to be Mahler's point of view.) Briefly stated, it is as follows. For the development of a solid sense of self—a healthy narcissism—a child needs an atmosphere of regard and respect for his feelings. Mothers who are emotionally insecure maintain their own narcissistic equilibrium by having the child behave in a particular way, a way not necessarily congruent with the child's own feelings. Children perceive this and respond by unconsciously taking on this assigned role and developing a "false self." Character traits and symptoms are manifestations of one aspect or another of their struggle to maintain this false self and keep their true self undeveloped and out of the realm of conscious experience.

This kind of formulation, in which all psychopathology is seen as originating in one particular aspect of psychic development, lends itself to being simplistic. There are certain patients who, in a very broad way, can be seen as having been treated by their parents primarily as narcissistic objects, whose parents failed to empathize with and to give validity to their own feelings rather than their compliant behavior, patients in whom all of this has led to a central problem very aptly conceptualize in terms of a true and a false self. But all of us have had to disown and repress some of our feelings, without any major self-impairment. To conceptualize everyone's problems as originating in this area is certainly an overgeneralization.

[[RunningHead]]

Foundations of Family Therapy: A Conceptual Framework for Systems Change

By Lynn Hoffman. New York: Basic Books, 1981, xv + 377 pp., $20.00.

Peter Martin M.D.

Peter Martin

Peter A. Martin, M.D.
Bloomfield Hills, Michigan

Peter Martin  

This book has been hailed by the field of family therapy as a muchneeded, timely, and important contribution. Lynn Hoffman is a recognized authority and leader. She attempts, as the title states, the overwhelming task of establishing foundations for family therapy through her conceptualization of systems and how to effect their change. In her conclusions she does not take an even-handed, broad perspective of apparently conflicting theories. Contrary to promotional statements, this book is not a synthesis of contemporary family therapy. It is a clearly and unequivocally presented synthesis of her own ideas, of what the present and future foundations of family therapy should be. Her incisive approach is to be respected and valued. It contributes to clarification of the reader's thinking. Whether her position turns out to be the wave of the future or just a current hobby horse will be known at some later date.

Epistemology is of great importance to Hoffman. In this emphasis, she follows Gregory Bateson's intensely moral concern for it and definition of it—the rules one uses for making sense out of the world. Hoffman's epistemology of family therapy starts with the idea of circularity as opposed to lineal cause-effect relations (as she labels the psychodynamic approach). The concept of circularity includes homeostatic and evolutionary paradigms. The homeostatic paradigm was the early (1950-1980) major systems model for the field of family therapy. Its emphasis was on homeostasis and equilibrium as illustrated by the cybernetic machine which always returns to a presumed steady state. This model was in keeping with the second law of thermodynamics. The evolutionary paradigm is a new template. Relevant to family theory, it considers living systems as having the capability of mutating to more complex organized regimes through small, usually random fluctuations. It is a model of nonequilibrium dynamics.

Book Editor's Choice

Otto Kernberg M.D.

Otto Kernberg

Otto F. Kernberg, M.D.
New York Hospital-Cornell Medical Center
21 Bloomingdale Road
White Plains, New York 10605

Otto Kernberg  

Medical Director, The New York Hospital-Cornell Medical Center, Westchester Division; Professor of Psychiatry, Cornell University Medical College; Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

WHAT FOLLOWS ARE DESCRIPTIONS OF RECENT foreign writings I have particularly enjoyed and benefited from. My pleasure at singling out for comment some of the many books that cross the book editor's desk is tempered by regret at having to keep the list within reasonable bounds. In presenting this selection I hope to share the excitement generated by encountering new ideas, fresh formulations, and additional applications of psychoanalysis. I also hope to broaden and deepen communication among psychoanalysts from different parts of the world.

David Liberman's Linguística, Interacción Communicativa y Proceso Psicoanalítico [Linguistics, Communicative Interaction, and Psychoanalytic Process] (Buenos Aires: Kargieman, 1983) is a challenging study of linguistic expression in various types of character pathology, as found in the psychoanalytic situation. Liberman developed an original approach to understanding the defensive functions of linguistic styles. I found particularly intriguing his recommendation, for example, that the analyst be prepared to frame his interpretations in a stylistic mode that is different from the patient's, and to avoid the patient's exercise of control by means of his linguistic style. Liberman's untimely death in 1983 was a great loss to psychoanalysis. It is to be hoped that this stimulating work will find its way into English.

11

[[RunningHead]]

Generations of the Holocaust

Edited by Martin S. Bergmann and Milton E. Jucovy. New York: Basic Books, 1982, 338 pp., $18.95.

Howard Levine M.D.

Howard Levine

Howard B. Levine, M.D.
Brookline, Massachusetts

Howard Levine  

For many years, the Group for the Psychoanalytic Study of the Effects of the Holocaust on the Second Generation has been conducting an in-depth, case-oriented investigation of the analytic treatment of children of survivors. Their work, which has focused on three related questions—(1) Is there something unique about the personalities of

708

children of survivors? (2) Is there something different about the process of their treatment? (3) Is trauma related to the Holocaust experience of the parents transmitted to the children and, if so, how?—has stimulated analytic interest in this area, made significant contributions to our analytic understanding of the complexities involved in the diagnosis and treatment of children of survivors, and provided insights into the difficulties posed by the analyst's reactions to the horrors of the Holocaust. It has also held the potential of extending beyond the examination of the emotional consequences of massive social, physical, and psychic trauma to shed light on many unsettled issues of character formation. Among these have been problems of superego development, particularly in situations of disrupted or perversely distorted social and parental value systems; the effects of trauma on the development and preservation of parenting capacities; the impact of intense parental narcissistic involvement or prolonged or incomplete parental mourning on the emotional development of the child; and the transgenerational transmission and consequences of severe traumatic states.

Generations of the Holocaust attempts to broaden and continue these important clinical and theoretical studies. For those not already familiar with the historical background of the psychiatric and psychoanalytic research on Holocaust survivors and their children, this book provides a concise and informative introductory section ("Prelude"), an interesting review of the persecutory practices of the West German government in the application of its Indemnification Laws (Chapter 3) and an extensive, although not complete, bibliography. The main body of the book consists of sections on "The Survivors' Children," which includes a revised version of Judith Kestenberg's paper, "Psychoanalyses of Children of Survivors from the Holocaust: Case Presentation and Assessment" (This Journal, 28:775-804, 1980); chapters on "Recurrent Problems in Treatment," "Hysterical Features" and "Superego Pathology," contributed by Martin Bergmann, Marian Oliner, and Maria Bergmann, respectively; and a section on "The Persecutors' Children," the latter being the first contributions to this subject to appear in the English-language analytic literature.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Concept of Psychic Reality: Its Meaning and Value

Robert Wallerstein M.D.

Robert Wallerstein

Robert S. Wallerstein, M.D.
Langley Porter Institute
401 Parnassus Avenue
San Francisco, California 94143

Robert Wallerstein  

THE ESSENCE OF PSYCHOANALYSIS CAN BE conceptualized from a variety of perspectives. One is in terms of its insistent dedication to the constant elucidation of the distinction between fantasy and reality. Hartmann (1956), in distinguishing various meanings of the concept of reality testing, underscored that one meaning or usage referred to the ability to discern subjective and objective elements in our judgment of reality. Arlow (1969) said of this: "Learning to do this is an unending process. Essentially this is the principal task which the analyst poses to his patient" (p. 29, italics added). Central to our formulation of this distinction from the very beginning has been our understanding of our much-used construct, psychic reality, and in this sense this concept has long been one of the fundamental building blocks of psychoanalytic thinking and activity.

Presented at the panel, "Perspectives on the Nature of Psychic Reality," at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 17, 1982. Accepted for publication December 12, 1983.

[[RunningHead]]

Obesity

Laila Karme M.D.

Laila Karme

Laila Karme, M.D.
Pasadena, California

Laila Karme  

Edited by Albert J. Stunkard. Philadelphia: Saunders, 1980, 470 pp., $28.00.

Obesity is a complex medical and psychosocial phenomenon. There is an abundance of empirical observations, new findings, and a growing number of theories on the subject. Many specialists in research or clinical practices may not be fully aware of important work in related fields. Dr. Stunkard, a recognized authority on the subject of obesity, has attempted to integrate these different areas, bringing together descriptions of the skills and attitudes of those working in the field. The first section, "Basic Mechanisms," explores the genetic, physiological, biochemical, pharmacological, and experimental psychological theories about obesity. This is an admirable attempt to present many current theories and controversies concerning them. This section is no light reading. It is technical and demands considerable effort from the reader. The second section is devoted to "Treatment" and examines the different treatment modalities available. This covers a wide range from a flow chart "algorithm," which is ahelpful practical tool to both physician and patient, to chapters on diet, physical activity, drugs, surgical procedures, self-help groups, social environment, behavior modification, and psychoanalysis and psychotherapy. There are also chapters on obstetrical problems in obese patients and on obesity in childhood.

Of special interest is a chapter on behavior modification, which provides a summary of the current status of this modality of treatment. Unfortunately, the results are short-term, variable, and rather disappointing—especially in the area of maintenance of weight loss.

162

[[RunningHead]]

Freud as a Writer

By Patrick Mahony. New York: International Universities Press, 1982, xii + 277 pp., $20.00.

Stanley Goodman M.D.

Stanley Goodman

Stanley Goodman, M.D.
Berkeley, California

Stanley Goodman  

Rereading Freud is a lifetime pursuit for students of psychoanalysis. As with other profoundly influential authors, however gratifying and illuminating an experience it may be to read him initially, his substance as a thinker and his power as a writer are increasingly appreciated as we return to him in the course of our own development. Every analyst later in his career has had the experience of reading a somewhat different Freud than he did as a beginning student; obviously the change is in the reader, responding differently with greater experience and enhanced insight.

Our continuing study is, indeed, a pursuit of Freud in which we may hope to go beyond the mere acquisition of an inventory and chronology of his ideas to be accepted or refuted. As we continue our individual conversations with him through his written work, attempting to comprehend the essence as well as the particulars of his contribution, every suggestion that may allow us to approach him more closely is appreciated. Patrick Mahony, a psychoanalyst and Professor of English Literature at the University of Montreal, in a remarkably interesting and instructive book, offers us just such a suggestion and demonstrates his own imaginative application of it.

Mahony argues effectively that attention to Freud in his activity as a writer can well repay our efforts with further clarification of him as a thinker, and that literary analysis can significantly supplement the conceptual analysis, extending our understanding of the thoughts through careful attention to the style and mode of their presentation.

663

[[RunningHead]]

The Strategy Of Life: Teleology And Mechanics In Nineteenth-century German Biology

Darius Ornston M.D.

Darius Ornston

Darius Ornston, M.D.
Greenville, South Carolina

Darius Ornston  

By Timothy Lenoir. Boston: Kluwer, 1982, 314 pp., $60.00.

158

Freud said that in order to understand someone we need to feel our way into their lives, to develop some sense of where they are coming from and what they are after. The strange thing is that we are so uninformed about the scientific tradition in which Freud developed and then, late in his career, invented psychoanalysis. Timothy Lenoir has written a very good book about German biology at the beginning of the 19th century.

Lenoir shows that Romantic Medicine was one stream in a tide of many "Nature Philosophies" which dominated German medicine and biology. They shared a firm belief that there were organizing principles common to all forms of life which could be intuited while studying an intact system. This clear and fruitful assumption powered the work of some accomplished biologists and physicians who searched for the characteristic relations of organic parts to one another, for what Johann Christian Reil called "purposeful order" or "efficient organization" (zweckmässige Ordnung). Lenoir has discerned a need, which all scientists probably feel, but which became unfashionable and went underground two generations before Freud's time.

When we break up a functioning system, it is much easier to prove something about a few variables, but we no longer are able to understand how the whole thing worked. Freud's distaste for experiment and therapeutic intervention flows in the observing and describing research tradition of Johannes Müller's physiology. As Freud worked out his own kind of science, he clearly preferred Müller's stance.

Another "Romantic" assumption Freud used for a time was the notion that a person has some privileged insight into his own mental organization and therefore into the inherent nature of everything else through a kind of intellectual intuition—from the "inside," as it were. Karl Friedrich Burdach's "self intuition" (Selbstanschauung) survived in Freud's early assumption about what he called "endopsychic perception." Freud silently changed his mind about this conceit as he came to see that our unconscious is just as unknowable to direct experience as is our surround. We do form ideas about both, but, by definition, we can never perceive the unconscious any more than we can mentally grasp a thing itself.1

[[RunningHead]]

Cognitive Aspects of Splitting and Libidinal Object Constancy: A Reconsideration and Reappraisal

Irving Steingart Ph.D.

Irving Steingart

Irving Steingart  

Melito (1983) advanced ideas in this Journal on how to "interrelate aspects of cognitive development as described by Piaget, with the development of libidinal object constancy described by Mahler and her co-workers" (p. 515). This commentary corrects what I believe to

974

be misapplications in the use of Piaget's ideas by Melito, to the detriment of both theories, Piagetian and psychoanalytic.

Melito's theoretical argument makes use of a change in a young child's cognitive ability which is described by Piaget (1947) as a change from "preoperational" (or "preconceptual") thinking to thinking which is "intuitive." This change in cognitive capability begins to become evident at about the age of four. Melito uses a procedure devised by Piaget to assess the concept of quantity. A four- to six-year-old, intuitive-thinking child, shown two beakers with the same amount of water, but one, A, long and skinny, another, B, short and wide, will center on either height or width and will declare either A or B contains more water. If the intuitive-thinking child has centered on height, this conclusion will persist if beaker A is made successively thinner. But a point will be reached when the intuitive-thinking child will say beaker A now has less water in it than beaker B because it is "skinnier" than B. The younger, one-and-a-half-to four-year-old child will never change centering and reach such a conclusion.

Intuitive thought involves not simply a single centration, as does preconceptual thought, but rather two successive centerings. As a result, intuitive thought involves greater coordination of perceptions and of images… However, the child does not yet reason with respect to both centerings simultaneously; that is, the child does not yet integrate the successive centerings into a unified whole [Melito, 1983, p. 526, italics added].

I agree with Melito that the integrative achievement of a psychoanalytically conceived object constancy cannot occur with the resolution of the rapprochement crisis (Steingart, 1969), (1983), but I disagree with his analysis and alternative conclusions.

[[RunningHead]]

Linking Objects and Linking Phenomena

By Vamik D. Volkan. New York: International Universities Press, 1981, 398 pp., $32.50.

Dan Buie M.D.

Dan Buie

Dan H. Buie, M.D.
Wellesley Hills, Massachusetts

Dan Buie  

With this book Volkan makes useful contributions to theoretically understanding and clinically approaching pathological grief. He has studied the subject intensively for 15 years, treating 150 pathologically grieving patients, most in brief or prolonged intensive psychotherapy, a few in psychoanalysis. A number of colleagues have joined him in this study; some of their cases are used along with his for illustrations. A succinct review of what is already known and thought about grief reactions is presented as a prelude to his own findings and thoughts about the subject.

Volkan conceives his patients to be neurotic and precisely delineates two forms of their abnormal grief. One is "reactive depression," which corresponds with Freud's description of melancholia. It is characterized by a disruptive identification with an ambivalently regarded lost object. This mobilizes a sadistic superego, which results in both loving and hating, wanting both to preserve and to destroy the self as identified with the deceased. The other form of aberrant grief Volkan terms "established pathological mourning." It does not involve pathological identification. Instead, in this condition the patient attempts to retain a vivid positive tie to the lost object, albeit with some ambivalence, through formation of a loved and loving introject along with use of "linking objects." Linking objects are various tangible items associated in some way with the deceased person—perhaps an item that belonged to him, a photograph, or something symbolic of the event of his death. "Linking phenomena" are parallel but intangible means of preserving an illusion of contact with the deceased; special songs and repetitive fantasies would be examples. In the closing chapter, linking objects and phenomena are differentiated from transitional objects and fetishes.

691

[[RunningHead]]

Children's Dreams: Longitudinal Studies

By David Foulkes. New York: Wiley, 1982, 477 pp., $38.50.

Isidor Bernstein M.D.

Isidor Bernstein

Isidor Bernstein, M.D.
Great Neck, New York

Isidor Bernstein  

Contributions to the study of children's dreams are relatively infrequent. It

237

was therefore with pleasure that I greeted a book devoted entirely to children's dreams. In his introduction, the author, who is Professor of Psychiatry (Psychology), at Emory University, points out that neither students of child development nor research psychophysiologists who have studied REM sleep and dreams of adults have paid much attention to children's dreams. Foulkes has been studying dreams for more than twenty years, and is the author of a number of publications on sleep and dreams including several previous reports on children's dreams. In this book, he intends "to show that the systematic study of children's dreams has a great deal to tell us both about child development and about what our minds do during the substantial portion of our lives when we are asleep." In addition, he wished to compare the content of children's dreams at different ages with those of adults, and to inquire into the relation of dreams to intelligence, sex, fantasies, and personality.

There were two concurrent longitudinal dream studies of children made in a university sleep-laboratory setting. The same children were examined over five-year periods beginning at, respectively, ages 3 or 4 and ages 9 or 10. The premise of the project was that longitudinal studies would provide greater understanding of children's dreams than cross-sectional studies, especially if information about each child's waking life situation could be obtained. To achieve this, the researcher set up a combined sleep laboratory and nursery school.

The children were awakened on the basis of stages of sleep, as identified in the recording, and were asked immediately to describe the dreams they were having just before being awakened. The interviewer asked direct questions: "What were you dreaming about?" "What could you see?" "What people were in it; who were they; were you in it?"

In years 1, 3, and 5 of the study, designated as "normative," the children were studied for nine consecutive nights with awakenings from REM sleep, non-REM sleep and from sleep onset. In addition, pre-awakening stimuli of a puff of cotton rubbed on the cheek, induced limb movement, or a spray of water on face or scalp were used to determine if such stimuli would appear in the dreams reported by the children. The children selected were from Laramie, Wyoming, an isolated Western community, the hometown of the University which sponsored this research. They and their families were described and rated by a comprehensive series of tests and observational procedures over a five-year period. Briefly stated, the children were mostly brighter than average sons and daughters of upper middle-class families.

Narcissus And Oedipus: The Children Of Psychoanalysis.

Marcia Cavell Ph.D.

Marcia Cavell

Marcia Cavell, Ph.D.
New York City

Marcia Cavell  

By Victoria Hamilton. London: Routledge & Kegan Paul, 1982, xi + 313 pp., $25.00.

The author contends that relatedness to objects, in some sense perceived as such, characterizes the human infant almost from the start. Narcissus is not a story about our earliest condition, but about that condition gone wrong. Though our adult conceptions of reality are derived—in particular from our earliest real interactions with others—neither relatedness to the world nor the desire to know it is rooted in some more basic condition or instinct. Oedipus is not a story about the sexual origins of curiosity, but about the tragedy of knowledge withheld.

These are claims with a dual nature: philosophic as well as psychoanalytic. Trained as a philosopher before she became a psychoanalyst, Hamilton treats the themes common to these two disciplines with originality and sophistication. And the alternation between clinical vignette and theoretical discussion give a welcome vividness to the latter.

Instead of dividing psychoanalytic theories into those which are "Freudian" and those which are "object-relational," as she calls them, she invites us to draw the fundamental distinction between theories of development that posit a primary state of isolation or union, on the one hand, and those that posit, on the other, an original diversity or duality. She maps psychoanalytic theories in the following way (p. 28): "Primary Narcissism or Mother-Infant Fusion/Union (Freud, Mahler, Kohut)"; "Primary Internal Object-Relations (Klein, Kernberg, Rosenfeld)"; "Intense Relatedness (Balint) or Primary Affectional Bond (Bowlby et al.)"; "Interactional Synchrony (Bower, Brazelton) or Mutuality (Winnicott)."

[[RunningHead]]

Couples in Collusion

By Jürg Willi. New York: Aronson, 1982, 265 pp., $25.00.

Edward Shapiro M.D.

Edward Shapiro

Edward R. Shapiro, M.D.
Belmont, Massachusetts

Edward Shapiro  

In his treatment of married couples, Jürg Willi's clinical observation is that despite their presentations of irreconcilable differences, they manifest complementary behavioral phenomena derived from similar areas of unconscious conflict. His book is an attempt to elaborate this observation.

Willi describes three dynamic principles for healthy partner relationships. First, in accord with Minuchin, he argues that there must be relatively firm but flexible boundaries between members of a couple, defining each individual, and around the dyad, defining the marital pair. Second, there must be a relative balance of self-esteem in the marriage. Finally, there must be available to each partner a flexible use of what he calls "progressive" and "regressive" responses to dynamic conflicts. This last idea is the key to understanding his notion of "collusion."

In pathological relationships, according to Willi, unconscious conflicts can be externalized, acted out, and rigidly stabilized in a marriage at times when partners both have similar areas of vulnerability and when they develop complementary and unconsciously collusive ways of managing their shared conflicts. This complementarity is manifest when one partner regressively acts out the conflict and the other uses reaction formation ("progressive") or other forms of denial and "adult façade" formation. Willi suggests, "They see themselves as irreconcilable opposites, when in fact they complement each other and create a whole."

Willi describes four patterns of collusion, variations of which he sees in all marriages: narcissistic, oral, anal, and phallic. In the narcissistic pattern, the behavioral division is between one partner who manifests the reaction formation of grandiosity and devaluation (with underlying ruthlessness) and one who displays the regressive picture of inferiority and idealization (with underlying wishes to entrap). The oral division is between nurturing (critical) and being nurtured (insatiable and ungrateful). The anal-sadistic pattern is one in which one partner is autonomous (tyrannical) and the other passive-dependent (negligent and noncommittal).

[[RunningHead]]

A Clinician's Guide to Reading Freud

By Peter Giovacchini. New York: Aronson, 1982, 251 pp., $25.00.

Arnold Rothstein M.D.

Arnold Rothstein

Arnold Rothstein, M.D.
New York City

Arnold Rothstein  

Giovacchini's A Clinician's Guide to Reading Freud might be reasonably retitled Giovacchini's Reinterpretation of Freud. The author's stated purpose in writing this book is to provide the uninitiated with a guide to reading Freud. His implicit goal, never clearly stated, seems to be a reinterpretation of Freud into a theory that is unifactorial and adaptational in its emphasis.

260

Giovacchini begins without a clear introductory statement of what guided his selections from Freud's larger work. The reader has to proceed to the appendix for a limited "Course Outline." It is clear that Giovacchini has read Freud closely. His summaries are good and informative with the exception of his review of Beyond the Pleasure Principle, which is poor. However, it is difficult to separate review and summary from commentary, hypothesis, and sometimes idiosyncratic reinterpretation.

This book suffers from being too personal and ambitious, which detracts from the author's fine distillation of many of Freud's works. In addition, it is written in a disjointed style that may derive in part from the book's origin in the author's transcript of a reading seminar. Finally, there are oversimplifications and inexact and, in my opinion, incorrect statements concerning the development of Freud's ideas.

Giovacchini begins with a discussion of "The Unconscious" because "the dynamic unconscious … was one of the most unique and magnificent findings of psychoanalysis." Chapter Two, "Studies on Hysteria" provides the reader with a good description of some of the differences between Freud and Breuer. He delineates their shared interest in psychic trauma and their theoretical differences: Breuer's hypnoid theory and Freud's beginning development of a conception of unconscious conflict and defensive repression. Giovacchini selects "Further Remarks on the Defense Neuropsychoses" because this paper represents a logical extension of Freud's interest in and emphasis on the dynamic unconscious. Giovacchini presents a good review of Freud's description of obsessional patients and Freud's conjectures concerning the relation between paranoia, hysteria, and obsessional neurosis. Chapter Four "On the justification for detaching a particular syndrome from neurothenia under the description 'anxiety neurosis,'" provides the reader with a nice summary of Freud's first theory of anxiety and its relation to nosology. The last six chapters of the book present Freud's ideas as developing from the perspective of energic instinctual theory to an adaptational object-relations perspective.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychotic Process

Nathan Segel M.D.

Nathan Segel

Nathan Segel, M.D.
Farmington Hills, Michigan

Nathan Segel  

By John Frosch. New York: Int. Univ. Press, 1983, XIII + 521 pp., $40.00.

In spite of Freud's recorded distaste for treating psychotic patients and the associated therapeutic pessimism inherent in his stressing the improbability of effecting a workable transference in the narcissistic neuroses, analysts have always shown an interest in unraveling the mysteries of the psychoses and tilting a lance at this therapeutic windmill. In recent years, important contributions have come from child analysts, like Mahler, on the one hand, and from investigators working with adult psychotics, on the other. Both groups have benefited from our pushing back the developmental frontiers through infant observations that have heightened our understanding of both early normal, and pathological development. John Frosch is among those who approached this study from a professional lifetime interest in the dynamics and therapy of primarily adult psychotic patients.

Encyclopedic though this book may be in covering the psychoses (there are some 18 pages of references), it is much more than merely a survey of the vast literature or an eclectic harvest of the views of others. In a scholarly fashion, yet peppered tastefully with a unique wit and humor, Frosch has attempted to make some sense of the divers psychoanalytic contributions to this data from their origins in Freud's own changing theories to the variations and opposing ideas espoused by other workers. This required some preliminary work before Frosch could even compare the above with the distillate of his own formulations. He was faced with a need to redefine terms such as ego and self, which cover a multitude of meanings, which in turn have been confused by metapsychological changes. Many terms that each of us takes for granted have been reexamined and redefined in keeping with his special and unified view.

143

[[RunningHead]]

Crowds and Power

By Elias Canetti. New York: Continuum, 1960, 495 pp., $17.50.

Martin Bergmann

Martin Bergmann

Martin S. Bergmann
New York City

Martin Bergmann  

The book under review is atypical for the review section of This Journal. It is neither recent nor was it written by a psychoanalyst or for a psychoanalytically informed audience. It may never have come to the attention of the review editor were it not that the author was awarded the Nobel Prize for Literature in 1981.

The book has, however, a claim on our interest since it covers the same ground that Freud dealt with in Totem and Taboo, the Schreber case, and particularly, Group Psychology and the Analysis of Ego. The subject of crowd psychology has acquired greater urgency since the rise of national socialism, and more recently during the Islamic revolution in Iran. The author is justified in claiming that modern technology with its rapid communication favors bigger crowds and more dangerous leaders.

This Nobel Laureate, a Jewish intellectual who grew up in Bulgaria, England, Vienna, and Switzerland writes about the subjects enumerated without ever mentioning Freud by name. Even LeBon and McDougal are unmentioned. That this is a personal animosity is evident when Canetti cannot avoid referring to Freud's Schreber case, but resorts to a circumlocution: "… a well-known attempt to find the origins of his (Schreber's) particular illness, and of paranoia in general in repressed homosexuality" (p. 449). The author claims that the destructive power of crowds has never been explained; he fails to mention Freud's explanation based on the abdication of the personal superego to the crowd or its leader. Equally amazing is the statement (p. 203) that the psychology of seizing and incorporating, like that of eating in general, is still completely unexplained. Thus not only Freud's Totem and Taboo is ignored, but also Levi Strauss's contributions. The author quotes in extenso and uncritically accounts of travelers to foreign lands, but no social scientist.

235

[[RunningHead]]

Thoughts on Meeting in Germany

John Kafka M.D.

John Kafka

John Kafka  

Many fears have been stimulated by the prospects of the IPA's meeting in Hamburg, and also hopes that professional and personal communication between German and other psychoanalytic groups will progress toward new levels of depth and ease. I have been asked to put on paper some of my thoughts related to this topic, presumably because I am an Austrian Jewish émigré, because I have had very close personal and professional contact with German psychoanalysts for many years, and because my personal feelings, thoughts, and experiences may parallel those of others. I am keenly aware that some German friends have traveled further than I have along the path of dealing with the past. For me, and I believe for some German colleagues, the increasing depth of our contact has, however, not always been accompanied by a consistent increment in the ease of our contact. We could, of course, gradually deal with topics which became accessible only because of our greater closeness.

In preparation for the meeting, I shall focus on areas of difficulty. We may be very sophisticated in condemning the concept of collective guilt, but as psychoanalysts we know something about the complexities of the development and maintenance of individual psychological boundaries and about processes of identification and their vicissitudes. There is no neat parallel or balance between the tasks that face individuals in different groups.

A German version of this commentary is being published in a new German journal, Forum der Psychoanalyse, 1(1), 1985.

Editor's note: This brief presentation by Dr. Kafka concerning the International Psychoanalytic Association meeting in Hamburg has not been published in English before. While it is not a letter, we believe its significance to the psychoanalytic community is sufficient to warrant publication in the Journal Letters section.

972

responseSet.title: Reply to Steingart
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Steingart

Richard Melito Ph.D.

Richard Melito

Richard Melito  

I am pleased to have this opportunity to engage in a dialogue around the ideas presented in my paper. I would also like to thank Dr. Steingart

977

for criticisms which have pushed me to clarify further for myself and others the ideas presented. Steingart makes three major points, which in summary are: (1) In normal development, rapprochement resolution precedes intuitive thought (the issue of "comparative development"), (2) intuitive thought does not enable rapprochement resolution, and (3) a concrete type of self and object constancy exists in latency.

Concerning the first point, I want to stress that the intent of my paper was to reveal the conceptual compatibility and complementarity of Piaget's and Mahler's account of cognitive-emotional events during the period under discussion. That is, the definition of emotional object constancy fits with that of intuitive thought. Specifically, emotional object constancy involves the ability of the child to overcome disappointment and rage in the face of frustration by remembering the loving behavior of the mother (see, e.g., McDevitt, 1975, p. 734). And, intuitions involve, for the first time, the ability to coordinate present experiences with "symbolic representations of past and present events" (Cowan, 1978, p. 161). In the language of centration-decentration, the intuitive child is able to decenter a present experience and recenter on memories relevant to that experience. As Piaget (1946) states: "… decentration of one relation consists of remembering the existence of others by reconstructing or anticipating them" (p. 168). Thus, the presence of emotional object constancy implies the presence of intuitions on the basis of the definitions of these two phenomena.

However, Steingart is correct in attributing significance to the issue of "comparative development." This issue, though, does not refute my argument; rather I view it as a phenomenon in need of explanation, which I shall now attempt to provide. Steingart proposes that intuitive thought is not relevant to rapprochement phenomena in part because "intuitive thought typically begins at about four years of age" while the rapprochement crisis typically ends before that age. However, it is important to note that the different age norms that are compared are, first, norms that apply in the one case to thinking about natural events (Piaget, 1947), and in the other to interpersonal-emotional events (Mahler et al., 1975). Piaget, in his notion of the horizontal decalage, has demonstrated that the functioning of a cognitive structure may be manifest at a certain age with respect to one type of task but may not appear until a later age with respect to another, even within the domain of natural events; e.g., the conservation of mass typically occurs a year or two earlier than the conservation of weight, although both involve the same cognitive structure, i.e., concrete operations (see Flavell, 1963, p. 22).

Borderline Phenomena And The Rorschach Test

Charles Peterson Ph.D.

Charles Peterson

Charles A. Peterson, Ph.D.
Chicago, Illinois

Charles Peterson  

Edited by Jay S. Kwawer, Howard D. Lerner, Paul M. Lerner & Alan Sugarman. New York: Int. Univ. Press, 1980, xvii + 505 pp., $23.50.

For better (as an incarnation of the living past) or for worse (as a stubborn adherence to internal presences destined to populate both analytic hour and everyday life), every transference involves an element of repetition, a compulsion to do to and to be done to as before. Surely there is an element of transference repetition in this important new book "dedicated to Merton M. Gill and Roy Schafer and to the memory of David Rapaport," the authors of the monumental Diagnostic Psychological Testing. Indeed, the reader can palpably feel the anxiety of their influence stalking the pages of a book which has repeated both the strengths and the weaknesses of its intellectual ancestor. Reviewers of Diagnostic Psychological Testing lauded the clinical wisdom, the theoretical brilliance, simultaneously apologizing for the statistical embarrassment. Change the names and the dates and we have—Presto!—a review of the present volume: an inspired interweave of theory and practice, coupled wth research that will convince no one, but "can do no harm.1"

This collection of original articles, written by psychoanalytically oriented psychologists, had its origins in a panel devoted to the use of the Rorschach in the assessment of borderline states at the 1978 annual meeting of the Society for Personality Assessment. Twenty-one loosely edited papers—all of which cannot be mentioned here—are organized in four sections: overview of theoretical and diagnostic considerations, treatment implications, research, and children and adolescents. Sections one, two, and four can be strongly recommended to the reader; section three can, at best, be regarded as promising, here treated with benign neglect. Mention of several chapters will illustrate the strengths and weaknesses of the volume.

The Legend Of Freud

Patrick Mahony Ph.D

Patrick Mahony

Patrick J. Mahony, Ph.D
Outremont, Quebec
Canada

Patrick Mahony  

By Samuel Weber. Minneapolis: Univ. Minnesota Press, 1982, 179 pp., $10.95.

This is a thoughtful book, although it will alienate many readers because of its arduous style and dense argumentation that cries for elaboration. The polyglot American author first wrote the book in German and then, with some modifications, translated it into an English that is at times strained and painfully complicated. I belabor this point at the ouset for this provocative work might well meet the fate of many other challenging yet ill-expressed writings in psychoanalytic circles. On the other hand, psychoanalysts, who are interested in a novel, bold interpretation of Freud and who are then motivated to read and reread many involuted passages will discover an author with a fine multidisciplinary awareness.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Odyssey. Psychoanalysis And The End Of Metaphysics

Arnold Modell M.D.

Arnold Modell

Arnold H. Modell, M.D.
Waban, Massachusetts

Arnold Modell  

By Stan Draenos. New Haven: Yale Univ. Press, 1982, 192 pp., $22.50.

The odyssey described here is Freud's intellectual journey. Draenos, who is a teacher of social science and essay writing at York University, approaches Freud in the tradition of Habermas and Ricoeur, that is, as a philosopher. An odyssey is not a simple and direct journey; and as intellectual history it is not an easy story to relate. Freud tells in "An Autobiographical Study": "My interest after making a lifelong détour through the natural sciences, medicine, and psychotherapy, returned to the cultural problems which had fascinated me long before, when I was a youth scarcely old enough for thinking" (S.E., 20:72). The cultural problems that fascinated Freud were, according to Draenos, religion and metaphysics. Freud may have set a task for the author, for he quotes a letter from Freud to Jung in July 1910: "I long for the lucid mind that will draw [from psychoanalysis] the justified inferences for philosophy and sociology" (p. 149).

Freud's well-known aversion to metaphysics, the author believes, betrayed its opposite, that is his attraction to philosophy which was later transformed, after a long circuitous path, into metapsychology. Metaphysics here is not taken as a term of denigration, but as a search for assumptions that are not to be questioned, a search for basics, such as the "essence of man." The author's thesis is difficult to paraphrase as it is complex, densely textured, subtle, and at times elusive. Draenos believes the advent of Darwin spelled the end of metaphysics and this was the start of Freud's journey. The end of Freud's intellectual journey was to "substitute science [that is, metapsychology] for metaphysics, to insert science into the void that the demise of metaphysics had opened in the cultural world of Western humanity" (p. 151).

203

Psychopathology And Adaptation In Infancy And Early Childhood

Roy Lilleskov M.D.

Roy Lilleskov

Roy K. Lilleskov, M.D.
New York City

Roy Lilleskov  

By Stanley I. Greenspan. New York: Int. Univ. Press, 1981, xvi + 263 pp., $27.50.

In recent years a number of psychoanalysts have entered the area of preventive intervention by taking part in programs that aim to avert or minimize later pathology by working with infants at risk and their families. A variety of different program models have been devised. They have included therapeutic nurseries, infant day care, and work with parents ranging from counseling and supportive therapy to focused analytically oriented psychotherapy. Those of us who have taken part in such programs know that existing theoretical frameworks only begin to orient us in this relatively unexplored world. Psychoanalytic developmental psychology, including the modern contributions, is of great help, but each of us has found it necessary to make use of the findings from the rest of developmental psychology.

233

Nathan Schlessinger M.D.

Nathan Schlessinger

Nathan Schlessinger, M.D.
Chicago, Illinois

Nathan Schlessinger  

By Emanuel Peterfreund. Hillsdale, N.J.: Analytic Press, 1983, 288 pp., $24.95.

In this book directed to clinicians, the author presents his personal convictions about the nature of the psychoanalytic process, based on his own analytic work and with a predilection for the language of information processing. He argues convincingly against stereotyped thinking and demonstrates the significance of early childhood patterns in a dyadic setting, but his theoretical outlook is more problematic.

In the opening sections, Peterfreund sets forth two approaches to psychoanalytic therapy—the stereotyped approach and the heuristic approach.

250

[[RunningHead]]

Free Association: Method and Process

By Anton O. Kris. New Haven: Yale University Press, 1982, iv + 113 pp., $16.00.

Mark Kanzer M.D.

Mark Kanzer

Mark Kanzer, M.D.
Harrison, New York

Mark Kanzer  

Kris appraises within this slim volume the methodology and processes of free association, foundations of traditional psychoanalysis that no longer seem as fundamental to many as they used to be. Perhaps the author's own sense of the complexity of his task is conveyed in the opening quotation from Wallace Stevens', Thirteen Ways of Looking at a Blackbird. Presumably there is some relation here to the thirteen chapters in the book. Actually, since the analytic method is described as a joint venture that blends the associations of the patient and the analyst, it must set off a myriad of potential "whistles."

The author discounts his own originality but nevertheless achieves it in the course of fulfilling his aim—to restate other basic concepts of psychoanalysis such as resistance, transference, interpretation, and insight, in terms of free association. The advantage in so doing, he finds, is that the latter process comes closer to clinical observation but tends to be subordinated to the other superstructures that are derived from it.

With the focus on free association, which the author equates with the process of psychoanalysis that is the outcome of the systematic use of the free association method, the goal of the analyst becomes the "enhancement" of free association itself. This is accomplished with the aid of his predominant neutrality and the establishment of patterns of continuity and discontinuity. Inasmuch as these may be found in areas of thought, wish, sensation, fantasy, etc., different analysts will lean toward diverse groupings among the associations, contributing to individual styles of communication between the partners.

A degree of organization among the individual constructions is obviously necessary.

706

[[RunningHead]]

Response to Melvin Sabshin's Commentary on Psychoanalysis and Psychiatry

Shelley Orgel M.D.

Shelley Orgel

Shelley Orgel, M.D., Chairman
Board on Professional Standards

Shelley Orgel  

June 16, 1985

I would like to comment on the article in the first "Commentary" section of the Journal (Vol. 33, No. 2), "Psychoanalysis and Psychiatry: Models for Potential Future Relations," by Dr. Melvin Sabshin. My opinions will concern both the paper itself and the selection of such a paper for publication at this time in the new section of the Journal.

The paper has many merits and is, I believe, worth studying carefully. It presents an excellent succinct history of American psychiatry and its relation to psychoanalysis in the past forty years. Dr. Sabshin's call for analysts to become concerned with such subjects as epidemiology, diagnosis, and the impact of third-party payments deserves a hearing and may stimulate constructive discussion and debate. His summons to reinvigorating collaboration and communication with psychiatry is timely, and I heartily endorse it.

The choice of this article to be first in the new "Commentary" section of the Association's official journal, however, seems to me unfortunately timed. And its arguments against nonmedical training, written in 1983, threaten to bring confusion rather than clarification among members of the American who read it in June 1985. As many know, in the six months or more between its acceptance and its publication, there has been significant activity within the Association to find an appropriate way to enlarge the candidate body to include some individuals without prior medical and psychiatric training. In early 1985, a proposal was formulated by a small committee chaired by Dr. Herbert Gaskill. This proposal would authorize our Institutes to accept, in consultation with a newly constituted committee of the Board on Professional Standards, a number of nonmedically trained applicants whose essential qualification to receive clinical training in psychoanalysis will be a demonstrable potential to achieve clinical excellence.

967

[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Melvin Sabshin on the Question of Nonmedical Candidates

Bernard Brandchaft M.D. and Robert Stolorow Ph.D.

Robert Stolorow

Robert Stolorow  

We appreciate Melvin Sabshin's (1985) candor in not attempting to conceal that his argument against training more nonmedical candidates is rooted in his avowedly political and organizational concerns. He believes that admitting more nonmedical candidates would lead to "a further separation and/or divorce" between organized psychoanalysis and organized psychiatry in this country, whereas continuing to limit such admissions would be in line with the strong, harmonious marriage between the two groups that Dr. Sabshin desires. And, indeed, he might be right.

What this argument bypasses, of course, are fundamental questions about the nature of psychoanalytic investigation and whether its essential methodological stance and mode of therapeutic action lend themselves to genuine conjugal harmony with psychiatry. Many contemporary psychoanalytic thinkers (Gill, 1976); (Klein, 1976); (Schafer, 1976, to mention just a few) have argued that psychoanalysis is, in its essence, an interpretive discipline devoted to the illumination of unconscious meanings and organizing principles and, further, that its healing power lies in the transformation of these psychological structures through an analytic dialogue (Atwood and Stolorow, 1984). In this characterization, psychoanalysis, in both its methodology and curative action, is pictured not as a branch of medicine, but as a psychology. What we wish to stress is that where psychoanalysis should be situated among the sciences of man is a question that should be decided by serious, systematic reflection on its mode of inquiry and implicit philosophical assumptions, not by restricting the number of its nonmedical candidates or by other such organizational expediencies.

While Dr. Sabshin grants that "a significant number of non-M.D.'s have conducted analyses with good technique," he feels that such favorable outcomes are outweighed by concerns about nonmedical analysts' diagnostic competence.

970

Emotion: Theory, Research And Experience

Theodore Jacobs M.D.

Theodore Jacobs

Theodore J. Jacobs, M.D.
New York City

Theodore Jacobs  

VOL. I: THEORIES OF EMOTION. Edited by Robert Plutchik & Henry Kellerman. New York: Academic Press, 1980, 399 pp,, $39.50.

This book, whose focus is on affect theory, is the first volume of a projected series on the topic of emotions. In compiling such a series, it is the aim of the editors to provide some integration in a highly complex field by bringing together the views of researchers and theoreticians from a variety of disciplines.

The present volume lays the foundation for this project. Divided into three major sections called "The Evolutionary Context," "The Psychophysiologic Context" and "The Dynamic Context," it provides a comprehensive overview of current thinking concerning the theory of emotions. By enlisting 14 contributors from a variety of disciplines who are actively engaged in investigating and thinking about the problem of affects, the editors have succeeded admirably in achieving their goal of presenting "broad theoretical perspectives representing all the historical traditions in the study of emotions."

Among the contributors to the book's first section, which takes up the evolutionary perspective, is Robert Plutchik who here presents his latest version of a model of affects first published some two decades ago.

253
