[[RunningHead]]

Introduction

Theodore Shapiro

Theodore Shapiro

Theodore Shapiro  

THE FOLLOWING ARTICLE BY DR. MELVIN SABSHIN inaugurates a new section of the Journal under the title, "Commentary." Articles contained in this section will not be authored by the Journal Editors. They will espouse an original point of view or reflect on an integrative theme. As such, the essays may stir interest, curiosity—but also controversy. In the case of controversy, we welcome members of the psychoanalytic community to submit brief responses that address specific points. It is anticipated that some of your answers will be printed in this new section. Thus, we hope to stimulate open dialogue among varying segments of our membership so that the Journal becomes a forum in which to discuss topics of current interest.

This first presentation aptly addresses the relation of American psychoanalysis to American psychiatry. I can think of no better spokesman than Dr. Sabshin, who is a member of the staff of the American Psychiatric Association and who is also a psychoanalyst. Publication of this paper in the Journal does not represent an espousal of his point of view, but surely is a testimony to his right to have a wider public audience—the readers of the Journal. In the future, contributions by prominent figures in and outside of psychoanalysis will appear in this section, and they will be as wide-ranging as possible. We shall consider integrative articles from workers in other disciplines who can enrich the analytic community with their vantage point.

November 15, 1984

Henry Lowenfeld, M.D.
New York, New York

Henry Lowenfeld  

The author of this ambitious book is a well-known German psychoanalyst. He holds the chair for psychoanalysis of the University of Frankfurt am Main, a rare distinction in Europe. A previous book by him found wide interest.1

Freud had the intention, writes Argelander, to write a comprehensive work about psychoanalytic technique, but he never fulfilled this plan. In 1904, he wrote that the details of this interpretive technique had not been described, and even the pieces of advice in The Interpretation of Dreams are only forerunners of such work.

Argelander thinks it is now possible to write it. He attempts to describe the cognitive process of analytic practice and to investigate the significance of the rules for it. We are inclined to react spontaneously in our practical work without trying to account for the process that takes place in the patient and in us.

Jerome Oremland  

This concise, clearly written book represents the distillate of the research findings from systematic analytically informed, cross-sectional and longitudinal observations of the sexual and gender development of the infant and young child. From these, the authors develop integrated hypotheses, some of which have been published elsewhere as individual papers, regarding early gender development.

Starting with Freud's acknowledgment of the incompleteness in our information about the prefigurements of genital sexuality, Roiphe and Galenson have since 1966 methodically gathered evidence enriching our understanding particularly of the second half of the second year of life and its relation to the development of sexual concepts, sexual activity, and sense of gender as part of the development of the sense of self.

They take that human dialectic which when studied as physical predominances we call male and female, when studied as behavioral proclivities we call masculine and feminine, but which from early on is subjectively experienced as an absoluteness of he-ness or she-ness and tease out its relation to the basic patterning in separation of self from object.

Using Margaret Mahler's extensions of Freud's basic phase-specific sequential developmental model, Roiphe and Galenson's laboratory, like Mahler's, is the natural nursery, their subjects infants and young children and their caretakers, and their instrument analytic eyes and interviewing with analytic knowledge. In this setting, Roiphe and Galenson made definitive observations which lead them to hypothesize that much earlier than even the boldest of Freud's assertions about the sexual nature of childhood, there is intense, specific, and gender-bimodally differentiated behavior which is an important achievement of the separation-individuation progression.

[[RunningHead]]

Intelligence and Affectivity

By Jean Piaget. Palo Alto, Calif.: Annual Review Press, 1981, 74 pp., $8.00.

Eva Lester M.D.

Eva Lester

Eva P. Lester, M.D.
Montreal, Canada

Eva Lester  

Despite continuous interest and despite his very prolific scientific output, Piaget never published a systematic account of his views on affectivity. The present volume is the first translation into English (by T. A. Brown and C. E. Kaegi) of a series of lectures on the relation between intelligence and affectivity delivered at the Sorbonne and published in 1954 in France.

Piaget makes two general statements in these lectures. The first refers to the nature of affectivity, best understood as the energetics (motivation) and regulatory function of behavior, while the second refers to the relation of cognition to affectivity. Piaget sees the two as interdependent and inseparable ("indissociable"), constituting two different, but complementary aspects of any act of behavior. Affectivity provides the force, the fuel, while cognition provides the structures that underlie all behavior. Piaget argues that affectivity as motivation and regulation may cause accelerations and delays in the development of intelligence; it may disturb intellectual functioning and influence its contents, but it can neither create nor modify structures. All structures, at least at the early stages, are cognitive in nature. In mid-childhood some affective structures are discernible for the first time. These relate to systematized interests and values and have their origins in the child's social relations. Moral feelings and the will appear later on as the structuralization of affects continues.

The major part of the lectures is devoted to the stage-by-stage outline of affectivity in parallel development with cognition. In contrast to the cognitive stages, however, the stages of affectivity are approached abstractly and theoretically. Only occasionally does Piaget refer to direct observations in children's affective expressions, and the reader cannot help but regret the incompleteness of this work. However, as Cowan points out, Piaget's ultimate aim was simply to put forward certain propositions, namely, "That there are systematic shifts in the form as well as the content of affect as the child matures … and that these shifts in affect are attributed to the structural properties underlying each new cognitive" (p. xi). In this aim, Cowan remarks, Piaget has succeeded, unlike Freud who, by placing the emphasis on the repression of affects since early infancy, fails to elaborate on the developmental transformations of emotions and motivation.

Frontiers In Psychoanalysis. Between The Dream And Psychic Pain

Leo Rangell M.D.

Leo Rangell

Leo Rangell, M.D.
Los Angeles, California

Leo Rangell  

By J.-B. Pontalis. New York: Int. Univ. Press, 1980, 203 pp., $25.00.

The key word in the title of this book is "Between." Although the author declares at the beginning that this simply corresponds to the order of the texts, the first being on the dream and the last on psychic pain, an immersion in the book's substance reveals that the emphasis throughout is on "the in-between." The focus everywhere is away from foci. Always we are asked to look between concrete nodes of material, whether data, observations, ideas, or theories, from the positive to the negative, behind any space-occupying psychic elements to what lies between them, or at the spaces they occupy.

Basic Problems Of Ethnopsychiatry

Eugene Mahon M.D.

Eugene Mahon

Eugene Mahon, M.D.
New York City

Eugene Mahon  

By George Devereux. Chicago: Univ. Chicago Press, 1980, xiii + 366 pp., $22.50.

181

This book is a collection of Devereux's papers spanning the years 1939-1966. These papers, rich in content, covering a variety of topics, anthropological and psychoanalytic, not only present a classic dissertation on the interface between anthropology and psychoanalysis, but also offer the reader a unique cross-section of the author's multidimensional mind.

As one reads and rereads the book, immersing oneself in the welter of anthropological data and their metapsychological implications, one develops a curiosity (a product no doubt of awe as much as of respect) for an author who dares keep his feet planted in two camps when most of us have difficulty enough keeping our balance in one. Devereux makes it clear that he is a psychoanalyst-anthropologist who has something important to say to psychoanalysts about culture, a multidetermined phenomenon that requires ethnopsychiatric as well as psychoanalytic definition. He feels that the psychiatrist should be as familiar with cultural concepts as he is with issues of defense, instinct, and structural theories, yet every psychiatrist might not be able, in his limited practice, to be knowledgeable about every nuance of culture. Devereux writes:

On a purely practical level, it is obvious that the average psychiatrist has no time to study in detail the cultural peculiarities of all who come to him for treatment—every Ozark mountaineer, reservation Indian, or Cajun. That is what the ethnopsychiatrist is there for. But the ethnopsychiatrist has an additional task … : the development of a culturally neutral psychotherapy, comparable to the nonevaluative emotional neutrality of psychoanalytic psychotherapy … [which] will enable the New York psychiatrist to treat with equal efficiency a refined Southern lady, an Eskimo seal hunter and a Filipino scout

(p. 90). Devereux coins the term "metacultural" for this culturally neutral therapy which in his opinion is "still in the making.

The Wolf-man: Sixty Years Later. Conversations With Freud's Controversial Patient

Arnold Rogow M.D.

Arnold Rogow

Arnold A. Rogow, M.D.
New York City

Arnold Rogow  

By Karin Obholzer. New York: Continuum Publishing Co., 1982, 250 pp., $14.95.

In the history of psychoanalysis no patient has been as famous, or as notorious, as Serge Pankejeff, Freud's Wolf Man. While Dora, lately reincarnated as a heroine of the feminist movement, is as well known, she as yet has not been the subject of two lengthy books. Nor did she write an autobiography, as far as we know, or submit to over 40 hours of interview. Dora, unlike the Wolf Man, did not make a career of patienthood, but no one, ever, is likely to equal the Wolf Man in that respect. From 1910 when he first saw Freud, until his death at 92 in the Vienna Psychiatric Hospital on May 7, 1979, the Wolf Man was in analysis, more or less, on and off for almost 70 years, a record not likely to be surpassed until human longevity is significantly increased.

The Wolf Man was unique in other respects as well. He was diagnosed, at various times, as obsessional, paranoid, depressed, anxiety-ridden, hypochondriacal, schizophrenic, compulsive, guilt-laden, psychotic, delusional, and narcissistic—a veritable nosology of psychiatric symptomatology. The number of his illnesses apparently was more than matched by the number of psychoanalysts, psychiatrists, psychotherapists, neurologists, and nerve specialists he consulted during the course of his long life. The roster included, in addition to Freud, Ruth Mack Brunswick, Muriel Gardiner, "Kraepelin" (I list the names as they appear in the book under review), "Bekhterev," "Ziehen," "Lubin," "Drosnes," "Dr. E., an Austrian analyst who had emigrated to the United States and was working at the Freud archive in New York" (presumably a rather thinly disguised Kurt Eissler), "Dr. S." of Vienna, "Dr. D.

The Psychoanalytic Study Of The Child, Vol. 36

George Kochis M.D.

George Kochis

George P. Kochis, M.D.
Houston, Texas

George Kochis  

Edited by Albert J. Solnit, Ruth S. Eissler, Anna Freud & Peter B. Neubauer. New Haven: Yale Univ. Press, 1982, vii + 588 pp., $37.50.

A reviewer of a previous volume of the Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (Olch, 1978) suggested that it would be helpful to have an epilogue essay to delineate some of the implied dialogues among the authors. It is not my intention to write such an essay, but as part of my review I would like to borrow the idea and share the potential dialogue I hear. Reading this volume, it struck me that a number of the authors dealing with manifestly diverse topics were each leaning heavily on the same developmental observation and conceptualization to support their theses. This unanimity struck me as noteworthy, and I could not help but continue to explore what it might mean. For the first dialogue I would bring together Brinich ("Ritual and Meanings"), Gaensbauer ("Differentiation of Discrete Affects"), Harmon, Wagonfeld, and Emde ("Anaclitic Depression"), Dowling ("Mental Organization in the Phenomenon of Sleep"), and Piaget. Perhaps Wolff ("Flying") should sit in as well.

Brinich proposes that the birth of meaning in communication is imbedded in the sensorimotor events in the early mother-infant interaction. The repetition of such events he calls "rituals," and these rituals take on significance of precognitive meaning. He emphasizes the activity between mother and infant as the "meaning-acquisition device" for the child. This begins with the feeding rituals in early infancy and continues to the time when rituals merge with words.

[[RunningHead]]

Narrative Truth And Historical Truth

Leonard Shengold M.D.

Leonard Shengold

Leonard Shengold, M.D.
New York City

Leonard Shengold  

By Donald P. Spence. New York: Norton, 1982, 320 pp., $8.95.

We live by metaphor and the mind is a poetry-making organ. (Lionel Trilling); and a myth-making organ; and a history-making organ. Past infancy we have a deep need for synthesis, continuity, and causality. The worlds within and without our minds may be inherently unknowable. But however relativistic and inadequate our senses and our memory, there is a reality out there and there are absolutes; there was a past and what has happened has happened, however imperfectly we have registered it and however impossible it is for us to communicate it or recapture it completely. Death — the idea of death — forces us to common sense that can violate philosophical consistency. After reading this book, I feel deep sympathy for that champion of common sense, Samuel Johnson, who tried to counter the metaphysical proposition that "the real" exists only in the mind by kicking a stone, saying "Thus I refute Berkeley." But Berkeley cannot really be refuted. Nor can his psychoanalytic descendant, Spence.

There is current controversy in psychoanalysis that is addressed in Spence's complex and contradictory book—controversy that, unfortunately, seems to drift toward extreme positions. Recently we have had the oversimplified "assault on truth" from Jeffrey Masson (Spence would call it an assault on "narrative truth," on the pathogenic power of our fantasies).

239
